nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef environments at depths of approximately 2–25 m, typically associated with host anemones on reef slopes and lagoons.

decorPreferences: host anemone (Heteractis magnifica or Entacmaea quadricolor)

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a wide variety of foods including frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, small crustaceans, zooplankton, and microalgae such as spirulina. Captive-bred individuals tend to accept high-protein pellets and flake foods readily. Offering a varied diet is generally recommended for long-term health.

imageCandidates.notes: Thomas Badstuebner for MDC SeaMarc Maldives; 5616x3744. Tack-sharp single portrait against blue water, vibrant orange body with the single white head band and the diagnostic black pelvic/anal fin clearly visible.
Mark Mancq; 4608x3456. Group of several individuals (adults plus juveniles) in a host anemone, vibrant and sharp, showing the species' social/paired habit and black-footed fins.
Jan Derk; 2000x1400. Pair over a magnificent sea anemone in natural reef habitat, sharp and well-lit, orange body with white head band and dark ventral fins.

summary: A striking orange clownfish with a single white head band and bold black pelvic and anal fins, found in the Maldives and Sri Lanka. Generally considered suitable for reef aquariums; tends to be hardy when captive-bred.
careNotes:
The Blackfoot Clownfish (Amphiprion nigripes) is endemic to the Maldives and Sri Lanka, making it one of the more geographically restricted clownfish species in the trade. It is commonly considered reef-safe and has been reported to associate with anemones such as Heteractis magnifica in the wild, though it may accept Entacmaea quadricolor or Heteractis crispa as substitute hosts in captivity. A host anemone is not strictly required for keeping this species.

Wild-caught specimens have a reputation for being sensitive to handling and shipping stress and may be prone to parasitic infections. Captive-bred individuals are generally considered more disease-resistant and are strongly preferred for aquarium keeping.

Like all clownfish, A. nigripes is protandrous, meaning all individuals are born male and the dominant individual in a group transitions to female over time. The species is best kept singly or as a mated pair, and has been reported to be aggressive toward other clownfish and conspecifics outside an established pair.
breedingNotes: The Blackfoot Clownfish has been successfully bred in captivity. Like other clownfish, A. nigripes is a protandrous hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as males and the dominant fish in a bonded pair transitions to female. Eggs are demersal (they adhere to substrate near the anemone), and brood care has been documented. Captive breeding is considered moderate in difficulty, and captive-bred strains are commercially available.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page. Provided adult size (4 in), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), minimum tank size (30 gal), care level (Easy), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), diet (Omnivore), reef safe (Yes), and native range (Maldives).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Pomacentridae, order Ovalentaria), maximum size (11.0 cm TL), native distribution (Maldives and Sri Lanka), wild preferred temperature (28.4–29.4°C), depth range (2–25 m), diet (omnivorous), and reproduction (oviparous, demersal eggs, distinct pairing). Temperature disagreement with LiveAquaria noted: averaged to 23–28°C for the aquarium range.

additional.name: Sustainable Aquatics
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum adult size of 4.33 inches (11.0 cm). Noted wild-caught specimens have poor reputation for handling stress, while captive-bred specimens are peaceful and ship well. Minimum tank >30 gallons.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 11 cm, minimum tank 100 L, native range Maldives and Sri Lanka. Noted species is delicate and sensitive during transport/acclimation, susceptible to parasitic infection. Listed as protandrous hermaphrodite. Minimum tank of 100 L averaged with LiveAquaria's 114 L to yield ~110 L.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been successfully raised in captivity, and captive-bred strains are commercially available. All individuals begin life as male, and the dominant fish in a pair transitions to female over time. Eggs are demersal and adhere to substrate near the host anemone.
